focus: R-colored vowels (/ɪr/ /ɛr/ /ɑːr/) — the American R sound in vowel combinations
focus_ar: حروف العلة مع R — صوت R الأمريكي عندما يتبع حرف علة
arabic_challenge: Arabic has a trilled /r/ (tongue tip vibrates rapidly). American English uses a retroflex /r/ where the tongue curls back without touching anything. When /r/ follows a vowel, it 'colors' the vowel — making a unique blended sound that doesn't exist in Arabic.

isolation.instructions: /ɪr/ = start with short /ɪ/ (as in 'sit'), then curl tongue back for /r/. The two blend together. Think: 'ih' + 'rr' = 'eer'.
isolation.instructions_ar: /ɪr/ = ابدأ بـ /ɪ/ القصير (مثل sit)، ثم اسحب لسانك للخلف لـ /r/. الصوتين يندمجون.
practice: ih-rr ih-rr ih-rr (blend them: eer eer eer)

sentence_practice: Come here, dear. I can hear you clearly.
Near here there is a career fair this year.
I fear nothing. The path is clear.
daily_drills.record_this: I am here. I can hear you clearly. This year is nearly over. I have no fear — my career path is clear. Come near and listen, dear.
daily_drills.self_check: In 'here' — does the word END with your tongue curled back? It should NOT end with a tongue tap or trill.

isolation.instructions: /ɛr/ = start with /ɛ/ (as in 'bed'), then curl tongue back. More open mouth than /ɪr/. Think: 'eh' + 'rr' = 'air'.
isolation.instructions_ar: /ɛr/ = ابدأ بـ /ɛ/ (مثل bed)، ثم اسحب لسانك للخلف. الفم أكثر انفتاحاً من /ɪr/.

contrast_with_eer.distinction: /ɪr/ vs /ɛr/ — mouth more closed for 'here', more open for 'hair'

sentence_practice: Where is the chair? It's over there.
I don't care — the air is fresh and fair.
Prepare to share — don't compare your hair!
daily_drills.record_this: Where is my chair? Over there, in the fresh air. I prepared to share my work. It's only fair. I don't care about my hair. I am aware of what's there.
daily_drills.self_check: Compare 'here' vs 'hair'. Your mouth should be MORE open for 'hair'. Both end with tongue curled back.

isolation.instructions: /ɑːr/ = start with wide open /ɑː/ (mouth wide like at the doctor: 'ahhh'), then curl tongue back. The longest and most open R-colored vowel.
isolation.instructions_ar: /ɑːr/ = ابدأ بفتح الفم واسع (آآآ)، ثم اسحب لسانك للخلف. أوسع وأطول صوت R.

contrast_all_three.distinction: Notice how the vowel BEFORE the /r/ determines the sound

sentence_practice: My car is parked far from the market.
It's hard to start in the dark.
My heart is in the garden under the stars.
daily_drills.record_this: I parked my car far from the apartment. It was dark but not hard to find. I walked through the garden to the market. The stars were beautiful. My heart felt happy. This is the start of something wonderful.
daily_drills.self_check: For /ɑːr/: your mouth should be WIDE open before the tongue curls back. Compare: 'here' (small opening) vs 'car' (wide open).

daily_drills.target_sounds: All three R-colored vowels in context

sorting_exercise.instructions: Sort these words into three groups by their R-vowel sound:

mixed_sentence_practice: I fear parking the car near here — it's too dark there.
Where did you hear that? I shared it at the party last year.
I don't care how far the star is — it's clear from here.
daily_drills.record_this: This year I started a new career. I heard about it at a party — my friend shared the news. I wasn't afraid to start far from here. Where I work now, the air is clear and the garden is dark and beautiful. I care about my heart and my art.
daily_drills.self_check: In your recording, find one example of each: /ɪr/ (year, career, here, clear), /ɛr/ (shared, where, care), /ɑːr/ (started, far, dark, heart, art). Are all three clearly different?

daily_drills.target_sounds: R in initial, middle, and final position (comprehensive)
daily_drills.focus: The American R in ALL positions — building toward natural production

initial_r.tip: Tongue curls back BEFORE the vowel comes

middle_r.tip: Tongue curls back briefly between vowels

final_r.tip: Tongue curls back AT THE END — hold it

r_clusters.tip: Tongue positions for /r/ even in clusters with other consonants

sentence_practice: I really ran around the room three times this morning.
My friend drove straight to the restaurant on the right.
I'm sorry — the street is very far from here.
During the morning, strong rain arrived from the north.
daily_drills.record_this: I run every morning. The road is really long — three streets from here. My friend drives me around in her car. I'm sorry I arrived late. During breakfast I remembered: the restaurant is on the right after the bridge. It was a strong, rainy morning.
daily_drills.self_check: Listen to EVERY /r/ in your recording. Is your tongue curling back for each one? Or do some still have an Arabic tongue-trill?

focus_areas: R in all positions: run (initial), very (middle), car (final), three (cluster)
Avoiding Arabic trilled /r/ — American /r/ = tongue curls, never vibrates
daily_drills.challenge_paragraph: I started my career near here last year. The market is far from my apartment but I don't care. Every morning I prepare, start the car, and drive through the dark streets. The air is clear and I can hear the birds. My heart feels free. I share my story wherever I go — it's fair to say this year has been hard but I'm not afraid. I steer my life in the right direction.
daily_drills.record_this: I started my career near here last year. The market is far from my apartment but I don't care. Every morning I prepare, start the car, and drive through the dark streets. The air is clear and I can hear the birds.
daily_drills.self_check: This paragraph has 25+ instances of /r/. Listen back: do ANY of them have a tongue-trill (Arabic /r/)? Every single one should be the American retroflex /r/ — tongue curled back, no contact, no vibration.

test_yourself.passage: Every year I hear the same story: learning English is hard. I don't agree. The truth is clear — if you start, if you care, if you practice, you get better. My friend started last year with fear in her heart. She could barely share a word. Now? She reads, she speaks, she drives conversations forward. Her career is far from where it started. She is near her dream. The air is different here — full of opportunity. Don't stare at the mountain — start climbing.

sounds_to_check./ɪr/: year, hear, clear, fear, here, near
sounds_to_check.Middle /r/: every, story, agree, truth, practice, better, forward, career, started, different, opportunity
sounds_to_check.Final /r/: year, hear, clear, better, her, for, far, near, here, air

scoring_guide.90_100: All R-colored vowels distinct. No Arabic trills. Natural American /r/ in all positions.
scoring_guide.70_89: R-colored vowels mostly correct. Occasional Arabic /r/ creeping in (especially in clusters like 'three').
scoring_guide.50_69: Still distinguishing /ɪr/ from /ɛr/ with effort. Some Arabic /r/ remains. This is NORMAL at week 5.
scoring_guide.below_50: R-colored vowels sound the same. Arabic /r/ dominant. Focus on Day 1-3 exercises daily until improvement.

daily_drills.progress_note: The American /r/ is the SINGLE hardest sound for Arabic speakers. It typically takes 4-8 weeks of dedicated daily practice. If you're at 50-69%, you are on schedule. Keep practicing — it WILL come.
